Sass]

should be faced by the disagreeable reflec
tion that our work in past years has failed
in its objective. We are not convinced,
however, that such is the case. If the
Central Council thinks otherwise, it would
be wisest to, defer its decision until after
fis momentous Whit-Sunday, on which
the spirit of the Collegiate Gacls is to be
put to such a decisive test. Some years
back a motion in favour of the College
movement was opposed tooth and nail
at a Provincial convention by a couple
of delegates who based their objection oft
the grounds that the Colleges were receiy-
ing too much “spoon feeding.” The ob-
jection was certainly a ridiculous one, but
the exbibition of selfishness which has been
given by “A Student” docs not make it
even quite so far-fetched—if “ A Student”
deserves to be taken as a typical Gaelic
Student. What rewards, medallic or
otherwise, did the pioneers of the National
Athletic ‘movement receive alter theit
strenuous engagements and contests in a
dozen different centres? Now, forsootb,
the Collegians ‘must get medals for one of
two fixtures, whether the money to pur-
cchase them ‘can be conveniently afforded
or not. Playing the Gaelic Games in this
spirit is infinitely worse than not playing
them at all, and if the spirit is a general
one—well, let us accept it as an effective
quictus to our efforts to Gaclicise the
student community. For eventual success,
a considerable degree of spontancity of
effort is requisite. We can do our best to
lead the Colleges in the way they should
go—but we cannot keep pushing them
along all the time. Otherwise they'd
form a very weik link in the chain of our
armory.
~ + +

Owing to the illness of the author, we
regret the interesting series of articles we
have been publishing upon the past history
of the G.A.A. has been interrupted for a
few weeks. We are. happy to inform
many of our readers who have’ written to
tus on the matter, that they will be con-
tinued as from next week. ft may be no
harm to remark in passing that because of
his opinions “Vigilant” has. been, and is
being, subjected to the meanest form of per
secution from political opponents, and
under the strain a breakdown in health
was, perhaps, inevitable, We feel sure
we Voice the feelings of our readers when
we wish him a speedy and complete re-
covery.

‘The Junior All-Ireland Hurling |
has come and gone, and Clare as
heir superionty over our boys in an’
Unmistalable manner. We were defeated
by a big score, and only for the fine goal-
Keeping of Carroll we would be con
pletely’ swamped. His play was the out.
Standing feature of the game—in fact, he
‘was the only maa on our team that playea
a fine game. Our boys were completely
on-plussed by the play and dash of the
Clare lads. ‘They had speed and stamina,
‘and played a nice combined game. Twas,
very much surprised at the display some
of our best hurlers gave. It was very
faulty and lacked judgment ; they seemed
not fo put heart into their work, that I
‘would attribute it to the fast mature of
the Clare lads" play. ‘They did not get
any chances. Some of them were played

downhearted at our defeat: “We know
that we went down before a superior
combination, but we will keep at it until
our efforts are crowned with success. |
Tho County Final. On May oth, the
County Final re-play will come off at
Portleix, and those who have had the
pleasure of witnessing the drawn game
played at Ballacolla some time ago did
not think for a moment that Ballygeehan
had gny chance of beating Kileotton,
much less to-draw with them; so. the
‘question at issue is an open one. When
they meet again, I expect to see a great
crowd present, for the game will be one
of the mightiest tussles for Championsti
honours that was ever seen in the county.
There is also another game of equal
importance to be played that day between
the old rivals, Rathdowney and Clonadee,
and the winners of this game will have
a great deal to say in this year's Cham.
pionship. Both teams are leaving noth=
Ing undone to deserve a victory, so the
struggle is bound to be a keen one.

JAMES LYONS’ GREAT RECORD,

Many of our readers who were interé
ested in athletes forty years ago probably
recall the remarkable record of James
Lyons, of Labbamologya, Mitchelstown
(now of Bank Place, Tipperary), who
shone in the seventies and early eighties
of the last century, in the time of those
great Irish athletes, the Brothers Davin
(Carsick-on-Suir), John Tobin (Ballydufl),
E, O'Grady, W. ‘Real, the famous Bob
Coll, etc. Mr. James Lyons’ first public
performance was at the Knockraha (Cork)
annual athletic sports on August 7th,
77, when he won the high jump, J. M
Murphy (Cork) being second. His
Victory in the high jump was a fortnight
later at that picturesque spot, Inniscar

on the banks of the Lee, where he won
the high jump from a big field, J. Burke
being second. At his home-coming, the
day after, he won first in the high jump
at Kantutk from Sherlock and Kelly, and
was second in th

hop-step-and-jump to
D. Looney. In 1878, his first appearance
was at Queen's College (Cork) sports
W. A. Kelly (Queen’s College)
Malone (Clare), and as Lyons

re spiked shoes for the first time he
ailed to come up to his usual mark. His
next and most successful day was at Ki
finane on the 15th August, 1878, where
he secured first prize in the s6lbs. off the

shoulder; also frst in the high jump and
in the running hop-step-and-jamp, and
tied with Mr. R. Coll in the long jump,

in which sixteen competed. Lyons and
Coll tied in the first round at 22 feet each

(off grass), and in jumping off the tie,
Coll improved his jump by six inches.
Coll could do more, but he stood back

from the take-off mark

In addition to his successes in. public,
Lyons has done many remarkable feats
in jumping. It was no trouble to him

to jump a horse any height. He often
jumped over one while tackled under a
common car, with straddle and britchen
and all on, on the hard road; and on the
field he could clearly jump aver a horse
with a man 14 stone weight lying on th

animal's back. He leaped all the grave-
yard stiles in the Mitchelstown aion
istrict in 1877 and 1878 after building
them, and he leaped over a wall at the
urn of the avenue to the lodge of Edward
O'Brien, J-P., Mitchelstown, which stands
six feet high, with a shovel on top. This
wall he jumped both in from the road and
Out also, which was considered one of the

most remarkable of his mony noteworthy
feats,

DUBLIN NOTES.

Dublin was again a quiet day in town as
far as fixtures went, as it was decided to
give all Dubliners an opportunity of wit-
nessing the Juniors do battle for their first
All-Ireland Championship. The crowd at
Croke Park, numbering about 2,500, was
composed principally of followers of the
Clare and Dublin teams, Mayo being al
well represented, while Leix had but a
small following.” Popular opinion prior to
the matches favoured the Banner County
and Mayo, but after half-time in the foot
ball contest’ Dublin were seen to be
strongly fancied, ‘The play in the hurling
match, with the exception of the first
fifteen minutes, when matters were fairly
lively, was of a disappointing nature, the
‘Clare’ men being immeasuraoly superior to
their opponents, the only redeeming feature
of the Leix team being their backs—Walsh
and Dunphy, who, time upon time re.
lieved the situation." It would be invidious
to single out any Clareman for praise, with
two exceptions, namely, their brilliant
young goalkeeper and Crowe at centefcld,

The play in the football match was fast
and scientific, the Mayo boys showing up
well in the first half, their fielding being
particularly sound, ‘and their forwarcs
deadly shots, but a lead of 3 points was not
enough at half-time with such a strong
breeze blowing, and in the second half
Dublin completely outplayed the Con-
‘men, thanks to their few week's
training. “The Emerald selection merits
the congratulations of all Dubliners for hav-
ing s0 easily disposed of an avowedly illegal
team, and it is to be hoped that hencefor-

ward country teams will remember their
county borders, and also the distinction
between the terms Senior” and ‘Junior
The’ best of the well-balanced Dublin
team were—Kearns, Kiely, Carey, Smith,
McDonalé, McAdam, Cromien, and
Synnott, who was unfortunate in getting
2 severe wrench in the ankle.

‘At Donabate Fingallians had the better
of their replayed championship tic with St.
Dulovgh, Innisfails, running out winners,
after a rough game, on the score—2-4 10
1-1. For Fingallians the outstanding
players were Howard and McKittrick,
Hickey, Coll, and Beggs did well for the
losers.

‘There is fine programme arranged for
Croke Park on Sunday next, the teams
Faughs and Rapparees in the hurling semi-
final, and Emeralds (the Senior All-Ire-
and champions) versus Parnells in the foot-
ball semi-final. Two good spectacular
‘contests are sure to take place, and I hope
to see every Gael in Dublin wend his way
to headquarters on Sunday.

‘As to. the matches opinions vary con-
siderably, but I fancy the Rapparees will
come out on top on Sunday, provided they
have the same team and play the same style
of game they adopted in their League tic,
for in that match both teams had out prac.
